evals.prompt: Diagnose this AI feature suite. We launched and only 4% of customers upgraded. Which failure mode?
evals.expected_output: Should run the four-failure-mode diagnostic. 4% upgrade is low and the diagnosis depends on context: (a) if customers say 'great features but not worth the price' → likely Feature Shocks (overloaded with features customers don't value enough to pay for); (b) if customers love features but the upgrade tier feels excessive → Feature Shock or wrong packaging architecture; (c) if customers say 'I'd pay for X but not the bundle' → Hidden Gem (specific features have higher WTP than bundle but aren't priced separately); (d) if customers don't engage with the features at all → Undead pattern (feature suite doesn't solve a real job). Likely diagnosis given 4% adoption: combination of Feature Shock (too much) and missing Hidden Gem (the 1-2 features customers actually value should be the upgrade trigger, not the whole suite). Recommendations: (1) Run urgent WTP study to identify which 1-2 features have the highest individual WTP; (2) Re-package as a slim premium tier anchored on those features; (3) Move lower-WTP features to base or kill them; (4) Test repricing on new logo customers, grandfather existing. Also coordinate with cx-jtbd to verify whether features map to validated jobs at all.

evals.prompt: Design WTP study for a Composable DXP services SKU we're considering. Pre-launch.
evals.expected_output: Should structure pre-launch WTP study: (1) Define the SKU concretely — what's in it, what outcomes for the buyer (faster time-to-market, lower TCO, modular flexibility, etc.); (2) Identify decision-makers — likely VP/Director of Digital, with influence from CIO and Marketing leader; (3) Sample target — 30-50 prospects across mid-enterprise and enterprise tiers, mix of greenfield and replatform contexts; (4) Method — start with monadic concept test (one concept + one price → buy/no-buy) at 4-5 price points across the sample; follow with conjoint for the highest-conviction segments to optimize feature/price packaging; (5) Sequence: Week 1 — concept test design, recruit; Weeks 2-3 — concept tests; Week 4 — synthesis; Week 5-7 — conjoint for refining (if signal strong); (6) Output — WTP distribution by segment, optimal price band, willing-to-pay-now vs. plan-to-buy-later split, segment-specific feature-bundle preferences; (7) Pricing model recommendation — likely tiered services (Good/Better/Best) or annual subscription with usage-tier add-ons; (8) Hand off to innovation-value-engineer for value case. Note: services SKU pricing is harder than product pricing because value is delivery-quality dependent — anchor on outcomes, not effort.

evals.prompt: Build the packaging architecture for an AI-native onboarding tool — Good/Better/Best, with rationale.
evals.expected_output: Should produce a 3-tier architecture with explicit feature ladder and pricing rationale. Tier 1 (Good — 'Onboarding Essentials'): core AI-driven onboarding flow, basic compliance checks, standard reporting, email support. Target buyer: mid-market with simple regulatory profile. Price ~$X/month. Tier 2 (Better — 'Onboarding Pro'): adds member intelligence (segmentation, lifecycle triggers), advanced compliance (KYC + BSA + adverse media), white-label, API access, priority support. Target buyer: enterprise with multiple member segments. Price ~$3X. Tier 3 (Best — 'Onboarding Platform'): adds custom workflow builder, AI training on customer data, dedicated CSM, outcome-based pricing option (lower base + completion-rate share), strategic account team. Target buyer: large enterprise or multi-brand. Price ~$8-10X (anchor tier; few buyers, but anchors the perception of Better's value). Add-ons (à la carte across tiers): premium concierge fallback (per onboarding), regulatory module per jurisdiction. Anchor placement justification — Best tier exists primarily for anchoring Better; the asymmetric dominance Better-vs-Good drives most upgrades. Hand off to innovation-value-engineer for unit economics validation.

evals.prompt: Our sales team is discounting 35% on every deal. Build the discount discipline system.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the structural causes (no discount governance, sales comp tied to bookings not realization, no segment-pricing logic, no published-price discipline) before designing the system. Recommendations: (1) Discount authority levels — sales rep up to 5%, sales manager up to 15%, VP up to 25%, anything beyond requires CFO/CRO approval; document in writing; (2) Discount-by-segment — published discounts only for declared segments (e.g., 10% for non-profit, 15% for multi-year, 20% for strategic logo), not freeform; (3) Discount-for-something — every discount earns: multi-year commitment (3+ years), public case study, named reference, annual prepay, or volume commitment; never give discount for nothing; (4) Floor pricing — set per segment, never go below without escalation; (5) Discount governance — quarterly review of every discount granted, root-cause analysis on outliers; (6) Sales comp redesign — move from bookings-based comp to ARR-realized + price-realization quality metric (vs-list); (7) Pricing committee — cross-functional (sales, product, finance, marketing) reviewing exception requests weekly; (8) Re-train: sales should focus on value-articulation, not negotiation-on-price. Note: 35% average discount means the list price is fictional — recommend either lowering list to reduce gap or holding the line and changing the negotiation pattern.

evals.prompt: We have a hidden-gem feature buried in the platform. Design a separate SKU.
evals.expected_output: Should structure the hidden-gem-to-SKU process: (1) Diagnose — why is this a hidden gem? Customers asking to pay extra for it suggests Type 1 (Profit Model) opportunity; verify with WTP study (5-10 quick monadic tests); (2) Define the SKU — feature scope (just the hidden gem, or expanded into adjacent capability), buyer (existing customers ready to pay extra, or new logo entry point), pricing model (add-on per-seat, separate subscription, usage-based); (3) Pricing — start at 25-35% of base SKU price (typical add-on premium); validate via pre-launch WTP study; (4) Packaging — likely add-on to existing tiers (rather than standalone SKU) to drive upgrade through existing customer base first, then standalone for new logo; (5) Sales enablement — sales must understand which customers benefit, why, and the proof points; (6) Pilot pricing — release to 20-30 customers at proposed price, measure conversion and ARR impact, refine; (7) Cannibalization check — does the SKU steal from existing SKU revenue? If yes, model net uplift, not gross. Note: hidden-gem SKUs typically generate 8-15% of company ARR within 12-18 months when executed well — significant upside, but only if pricing matches WTP.

evals.prompt: We're launching a new B2B SaaS in 12 weeks. Pricing hasn't been discussed yet. What should I do?
evals.expected_output: Should respond with urgency: pricing should have been validated 12+ weeks ago, not 12 weeks before launch. The Ramanujam & Tacke principle is WTP-before-product; this team is at risk of all four failure modes simultaneously (Feature Shock, Minivation, Hidden Gem, Undead). Crash-recovery plan: (1) Week 1-2 — emergency WTP study with 25-40 design partners and prospects; van Westendorp PSM at minimum, monadic concept tests at 3 price points if time allows; (2) Week 3-4 — packaging architecture (likely Good/Better/Best for B2B SaaS); pricing model selection (tiered subscription is default unless usage-based fits better); (3) Week 5-6 — pricing committee review, sales discount discipline drafted, sales enablement materials; (4) Week 7-12 — soft-launch pricing with 5-10 strategic prospects to validate, refine, lock in for general launch. Caveats: this is rushed; pricing decisions made under launch pressure are systematically worse than pricing tested earlier. Recommend the team accept that pricing may need a formal re-evaluation 6-12 months post-launch based on real-world signal. Future innovations should run WTP at Stage 2-3 of validation, not Stage 4.

evals.prompt: Our pricing model is per-seat-per-month. Customers say it's predictable but doesn't scale value. Should we change it?
evals.expected_output: Should treat this as a Profit Model (Type 1) re-design question, not a price-point question. Diagnose: per-seat is simple but caps revenue at user count, decouples value from usage (customers with high-value use cases pay the same as low-usage customers), and creates friction on user expansion (customers limit license counts to control cost). Alternatives to evaluate: (1) Tiered subscription with usage tiers — base price for X seats + Y usage, additional usage at incremental price; preserves predictability while capturing value; (2) Outcome-based — value-share or savings-share (lower base + share of business outcomes); high-trust required, high upside; works for select segments; (3) Hybrid (most common upgrade path) — base subscription + usage tier + outcome bonus for select customers. Decision factors: (a) Is customer value usage-correlated? If yes, usage tier makes sense; (b) Are outcomes measurable and attributable? If yes, outcome-based is plausible for a segment; (c) Does sales have the maturity for outcome-based deals (longer cycles, complex contracting)? Recommendation: pilot the new model on new logo deals first, grandfather existing per-seat customers. Run the redesign through innovation-typologist (Type 1 lens) and innovation-value-engineer (revenue model impact).

evals.prompt: We're considering free trial vs. freemium for a new B2B product. Which works?
evals.expected_output: Should differentiate the two and assess fit: (1) Free trial — full product, time-limited (14-30 days typical); creates endowment effect (Kahneman/Ariely loss aversion); converts when the product creates measurable value within the trial window; (2) Freemium — permanently free tier with limits + paid upgrade tiers; creates a funnel; converts when the free tier creates desire for premium features through use. Fit factors: (a) Time-to-value — if customer can see value in 7-14 days, free trial works; if value compounds over months, freemium better; (b) Viral or low-CAC dynamics — freemium needs cheap user acquisition (organic, viral) to make sense; if CAC is high, freemium loses money; (c) B2B vs. B2C — freemium works in B2C and prosumer; in B2B, free trial usually better unless the product is bottoms-up adoption (developer tools, productivity apps where users adopt before procurement); (d) Product complexity — complex B2B benefits from free trial with concierge onboarding; simple products work freemium; (e) Pricing realization — freemium often undermines later pricing because customers anchor on $0; free trial with explicit conversion deadline preserves anchor. Recommendation framework: default to free trial for B2B unless product has strong bottoms-up adoption pattern; freemium only with deliberate cohort design and conversion path mapped. Reference Ramanujam & Tacke on freemium failure modes — most freemium plays trap themselves at <2% conversion when 5-10% is required for sustainability.
